description: Creative user flow software providing analysis tools based on personas emotions

problem.headline: Problem Statement
problem.body: Develop a marketable strategy and actionable solutions to strengthen sustainable engagement, improving employee retention and attracting next-generation talent.

constraints.headline: Constraints
body: Use only predefined modules (Dining, Event Registration, etc.)
Easily implementable and configurable within backend systems
Include trackable metrics for KPI monitoring
Translate technical features into business value

scope.headline: Scope Establishment
scope.body: Our team first defined the specific use cases for the framework to ensure efficiency and relevance throughout the project. We structured the framework around three key pillars, separating the mechanics and logic from the visual design to allow greater flexibility based on client budget, brand identity, and project objectives.

workflow.headline: Integration Within Practical Workflow
body: As my responsibility was to focus on the technical and practical application of the framework rather than sales promotion, I quickly recognized the need to bridge and validate strategic choices with tangible, measurable data.

Designing a game economy is inherently iterative and requires long-term tracking to better analyze patterns and identify weak points. I found it valuable to develop a predictive modeling tool to assess user flow balance in advance and prevent potential UX issues such as friction or low rewards. As a result, as part of R&D, I initiated a user flow analysis software tailored to the project’s needs: the EMMA project was born.

brainstorm.headline: Inspiration Brainstorm
brainstorm.body: Since our project was closely related to gamification, it felt relevant to mix a 'professional look' with a 'table-top'-style game where strategy is defined using frames, modules, personas, and flows (VST served as a strong inspiration for blending highly functional interfaces with strong visual design).

physical.headline: Infusing Physicality
body: Since the software relied heavily on gamification, it felt natural to give the interface a more physical quality.

I drew inspiration from early-2000s interfaces like Windows XP and early versions of Mac OS X, as well as the tactile UI styles often found in video games. The goal was to bring a sense of material and playfulness to the overall interface.

industrial.body: A key idea was to balance a professional feel with a more playful tone. This led to experimenting with components in a way similar to industrial design by assigning them materials, and carefully considering how different shapes might suggest particular movements or uses.

documentation.headline: In-App Documentation
documentation.body: Given the complexity of the software and overall strategy, it was necessary to include a diegetic journal panel to gather formulas and documentation, helping properly educate the user.

gamified.headline: Gamified UI
gamified.body: We added higher narrative value through physical objects like 'pods' or 3D-based icons and personas. This 'gamified UI', similar to FigJam, creates a 'draft-and-try' tone that encourages productivity.

persona.headline: Persona Definition
persona.body: For research purposes, we worked with a set of predefined generic personas based on various research documents and articles. For each persona, we defined key motivations and aversions to better understand their needs and drivers in daily life, particularly within a professional context, which was the initial target of the project.

engines.headline: Motivational Engines
engines.body: To ensure the project met its goals and remained flexible across different contexts, we created a clear distinction between the project functionalities (the exposed user interface) and the underlying drivers that connect these functionalities to create an engaging, holistic user experience. We defined three core engines:
list: Motivation: engage users through extrinsic rewards
Emotional: engage users through personal engagement
Social: engage users through social belonging
engines.end: By dividing decision drivers into distinct goals, we ensure a more targeted and balanced approach to the mechanics.

expressivity.headline: Expressivity
expressivity.body: Early on, we recognized the need for the system to be flexible according to clients’ organizational culture, stakeholder preferences, and employee personas, which vary widely across industries. We created a two-axis graph to map system adaptability and identify where gamification could be expressed.

module.headline: Module-Action Model
module.body: As our concepts matured, we aimed to bridge them with practical solutions. This led to the Module-Action Model: a module-based infrastructure where each module acts as an interface with predefined actions, configurable and linkable to any system via event hooks or similar mechanisms, allowing us to translate human emotions and behavior into trackable data.

architecture.headline: Data Modeling
architecture.body: The next challenge was defining the behavioral, temporal, and motivational properties of modules and actions. Modules own actions and actions could be clicks or swipes, deliver rewards, and be available temporarily or permanently. All elements are configurable, leading to the first flow of the Module ↔ Action relationship with their respective properties.

intro.headline: Weighted-Sum Approach
intro.body: After clarifying the metrics we wanted to track and the triggers for them, we focused on defining fundamental formulas to bridge user emotions with tangible, actionable data. To keep the system flexible and configurable, we used a weighted sum approach, allowing precise control over how each data point impacts a specific motivation.

impact.headline: Penalty System
impact.body: We explored how to translate and correlate emotions into factors, which led to defining a penalty system based on whether a persona responded to a particular emotional drive.

tweaking.headline: Normal Distribution
tweaking.body: During real-case scenario testing, we noticed some formulas did not yield the expected results. The main issue was the normalization function, so we switched from a linear to a logarithmic normalization to improve accuracy.

software_architecture.headline: Software Architecture
software_architecture.body: Once the formulas were set, it became intuitive to define the key software functionalities needed to model our projections through user flow.

resource_manager.headline: Resource Manager Model
resource_manager.body: A resource manager coupled with an ID registry is used to handle resource lifecycles, from creation to destruction.

limits.heading: Limits
items: Sparse spatial locality if use linked list for dynamic capacity
Slight overhead instead of using pointers

state_array.headline: State-Based Array
state_array.body: A cache-friendly, state-based array approach was implemented to process state-shared objects contiguously.

benefits.heading: Benefits
items: Greater data locality and cache hits

limits.heading: Limits
items: Less intuitive that Enum or boolean based state

semantics.headline: Semantics
semantics.body: To clarify each function's and object's purpose and usage (hot or cold path), clear semantics were established, ensuring optimizations occur in the right places, such as preventing branch misprediction in hot loops.

ui_caching.headline: Data Caching
ui_caching.body: For this project, we used an immediate-mode UI library to explore its limitations and potential workarounds. Since this approach redraws the entire interface every frame, it can introduce notable performance costs. To mitigate this, we developed a dedicated widget lifecycle flow that improves organization and scalability, even if it doesn’t fully resolve the underlying limitation.

ui_splitting.headline: Domain Splitting
ui_splitting.body: Finally, since the choice of interface library may change based on the results of this initial experiment, we decided to separate the domain from the visual presentation. This means core data is independent from the visual and interaction behaviors.

heatmap_flow.headline: Leverage WebGPU For Heatmap Computation
heatmap_flow.body: Early on, we identified the need for a heatmap system to analyze user flow strengths, weaknesses, and potential emotional impacts. To implement this, we established a WebGPU compute and render pipeline. The main challenge was integrating custom shaders with the existing UI library rendering system.

brand.headline: A Branded Solutions
brand.body: We consolidated all previous research and solutions into an official branded framework, enhancing credibility and making the solution more memorable and engaging during client presentations.

analysis.headline: Strategic Cultural Analysis
analysis.body: We developed a methodology to predefine the most suitable aesthetic direction or module package based on company needs, industry, and budget (CVF/Complex).

tools.headline: In-House Tools Ecosystem
tools.body: Other tools were developed to accelerate design system creation and export for development. The overall ecosystem connects multiple tools to enhance efficiency and productivity:
list: Style Studio: A figma plugin to quickly generate and fine tune colors schemes or fonts
SpriteGen: A plugin to quickly export a sprite atlas into a texture with generated coordinates and enums for faster synchronisation and lookup during development time

css2h: As Style Studio doesn’t support C/C++ style token export,css2h is a python script that turns CSS variables (colors and numbers) into a C style Header with enums se we can directly reuse it in our software environment.

software.headline: Userflow Analysis Software
software.body: A key outcome of this project was the development of EMMA (Emotional Mapper), a user flow software that evaluates flow efficiency based on various metrics such as excitement and social bonding.

heading: Decoupled Core
Development Complexity
costs: Ownership can be confusing (who owns what)

heading: Immediate-mode UI
Long-term Scalability
costs: Tight coupling between frame logic and rendering

heading: Solo Development
Production Readiness
costs: Limited peer review (regret some architecture decisions)

heading: Formula Flexibility
Interpretability
benefits: Requires documentation that adds transparency for all stakeholders

outcomes.headline: Outcomes & Learning
body: For larger, production-level projects, choose a more suitable UI framework, potentially even a JavaScript-based one, especially for web applications like EMMA.

Technical explorations can lead down a rabbit hole. Always track the initial business goals and coordinate with stakeholders to ensure the project aligns with market needs and company direction. For this project, I was fortunate to collaborate with a talented, market-oriented colleague who helped keep the project on track while continuously contributing fresh ideas.
